mainEntity.name: What camera should I take on a photography holiday?

acceptedAnswer.text: The one you already know. Travel rewards fluency with your kit, not theoretical sensor quality. If buying, an APS-C or full-frame mirrorless with a 24-105mm zoom covers 90% of scenarios in the lightest package.

For most travel, yes. A 24-105mm or 24-120mm zoom covers wide landscapes, portraits, street scenes, and food details. You'll photograph more overall because you're not changing lenses or leaving kit at the hotel.

mainEntity.name: Can I do travel photography on my phone?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, entirely. Modern phones are excellent for street, food, interiors, and daylight landscapes. Limitations are low-light and reach. For city-focused holidays, a phone is a legitimate travel camera.

mainEntity.name: How do I photograph people respectfully while travelling?

acceptedAnswer.text: Ask first for posed portraits. Chat for two minutes after they say yes — the photograph after that is immeasurably better. For candid street, make people one element of a wider story. Never photograph children without parent consent.

mainEntity.name: How do I keep gear safe in unfamiliar places?

acceptedAnswer.text: Plain-looking bag, short cross-body strap, nothing visible in car boots. Split kit across bags. Back up daily. Check travel insurance covers your gear value — standard policies cap at £1,500 and may need a photographic add-on.

mainEntity.name: What's the best way to back up photos on the road?

acceptedAnswer.text: Two-device rule minimum. Options: laptop + portable SSD, portable card-to-SSD reader, or cloud (needs reliable WiFi). Whichever you choose, every night, no exceptions. Lost cards and stolen laptops happen eventually.

mainEntity.name: Do I need a tripod for travel?

acceptedAnswer.text: Usually no — modern high-ISO lets you handhold at 1/30s ISO 6400 for most scenes. A travel tripod earns its weight only for long-exposure landscapes or interiors. A 200g tabletop tripod fits in a jacket pocket as a compromise.

mainEntity.name: How do I not spend my whole holiday behind the camera?

acceptedAnswer.text: Shoot with intention the first two hours and last two hours of the day. Put the camera away between. Stronger portfolio, actual memories. The every-waking-hour photographer comes home exhausted with 2,000 frames and 20 keepers.
